entities.name: PIGEONPEA
entities.description: A legume crop that has been the subject of breeding activities for about a century.

entities.name: EARLY MATURING
entities.description: The characteristic of reaching maturity quickly, specifically within 90-150 days.

entities.name: LATE MATURING
entities.description: The characteristic of taking a longer time to reach maturity, which historically dominated pigeonpea cultivation.

entities.name: BREEDING
entities.description: The process of developing new crop varieties with desired characteristics.

entities.name: SPONTANEOUS MUTATIONS
entities.description: Natural genetic mutations that served as a primary source for early maturing genotypes in landraces.

entities.name: TRANSGRESSIVE SEGREGATION
entities.description: A genetic process where offspring exhibit traits more extreme than their parents, leading to early maturity.

entities.name: INDUCED MUTATIONS
entities.description: Artificial genetic mutations used to produce early maturing varieties.

entities.name: HIGH YIELDING
entities.description: The ability of a cultivar to produce a high amount of crop output.

entities.name: ADAPTATION
entities.description: The ability of a crop to adjust to various environmental barriers.

entities.name: CROPPING SYSTEMS
entities.description: The organized pattern of crop production on land, which is diversified by early maturing cultivars.

relationships.description: Breeding activities are conducted on pigeonpea to improve its traits.
Breeding efforts were directed towards creating early maturing varieties.
Spontaneous mutations in landraces led to the origin of early maturing genotypes.
Transgressive segregation produced early maturing varieties.
Induced mutations were used to produce early maturing varieties.
Early maturing cultivars help in widening adaptation barriers.
Early maturing cultivars contribute to the diversification of cropping systems.
High yielding cultivars are often early maturing.
Late maturing cultivars historically dominated pigeonpea cultivation.
